source.title: The HSA is the only triple-tax-advantaged account in the code

ad_headlines: Legal. Sourced. The only account the code lets you skip tax on three times.
An HSA isn't a hack. It's an IRC §223 account doing what the code says it does.
One account. Three legal tax breaks. (Hint: it's not the 401(k).)
variants.twitter:
The HSA is the only account in the U.S. code with three legal tax breaks:
1. Contribution comes off taxable income
2. Balance grows tax-free
3. Qualified medical withdrawals are tax-free

Source: IRC §223, IRS Pub 969. Educational, not advice.
variants.linkedin:
The HSA is the only account in the U.S. tax code that gets three legal breaks. Most people I talk to know about one of them — and stop there.

Here's the actual list, sourced to IRC §223 and IRS Publication 969:

• Your contribution comes off your taxable income for the year.
• The balance grows tax-free.
• Withdrawals for qualified medical expenses come out tax-free too.

It's available only to people on an IRS-qualifying high-deductible health plan, and it's not a use-it-or-lose-it account — unspent money rolls over and is yours to keep through job changes.

The quiet part: an HSA you don't spend in your working years can function as a second retirement account. Once you hit 65, non-medical withdrawals are taxed like a traditional IRA — no penalty.

I keep coming back to this one because it doesn't ask you to do anything clever. It just asks you to use the rule the way IRC §223 wrote it.

Educational only. Not tax advice — verify current contribution limits and HDHP definitions against IRS guidance for your tax year.
variants.reddit:
**The HSA is the only triple-tax-advantaged account in the code (and most people use it like an FSA)**

Not a hack. Not a loophole the IRS is mad about. It's literally what IRC §223 says.

Three legal breaks on the same dollar:
1. Contributions reduce your taxable income.
2. The balance grows tax-free.
3. Qualified medical withdrawals are tax-free.

Requirements: you're on an IRS-qualifying HDHP, not on Medicare, not a dependent on someone else's return. Money rolls over forever — it's not an FSA.

The sleeper feature: after 65, non-medical withdrawals work like a Traditional IRA (taxed, no penalty). So an unspent HSA is a stealth retirement bucket.

Sources: IRS Pub 969, Form 8889, IRC §223. Educational, not advice — verify current-year limits against the IRS.

brand_voice_principles: Plain-spoken, confident, demystifying. Translates tax-code complexity.
A little irreverent about how needlessly complicated the system is — on the reader's side.
Specific and sourced. Every claim points to a real rule.
No: 'one weird trick', 'IRS hates this', guaranteed savings, sleaze.
